description: The way of the katana, wakizashi, and odachi. Governs all sword‑based combat, from drawing the blade (iai) to executing precise cuts and parries. Higher ranks unlock advanced techniques like iaijutsu (quick‑draw) and nitōjutsu (two‑sword style). A samurai’s soul resides in his sword; this skill determines how fiercely he defends it.

Mastery of the asymmetrical yumi bow. Covers foot archery and mounted archery (yabusame). High skill allows shooting at long range, in wind, or from horseback with deadly precision. The bow was the samurai’s primary weapon before the rise of the katana, and a skilled archer can turn the tide of battle before swords are drawn.

The art of the yari (spear), the most common weapon of ashigaru foot‑soldiers. Includes thrusting, sweeping, and keeping multiple opponents at bay. A well‑trained spearman can hold a line against cavalry charges. Higher skill enables you to parry arrows and fight in tight formations.

Skill with the naginata, a curved blade on a long shaft. Favoured by warrior monks (sōhei) and noblewomen defending their homes. Its long reach and slashing power make it deadly in open spaces. At high ranks, you can sweep multiple opponents and disarm enemies with elegant, flowing movements.

Combat with the rokushaku‑bō (six‑foot staff). A humble weapon that can disarm, trip, or shatter bone. High ranks allow seamless transitions between strikes and blocks. Many wandering monks rely on the staff both for defence and as a walking aid; mastering it is a path to inner calm as well as outward strength.

Throws, joint locks, and immobilisation techniques. Used when weapons are lost or in close quarters. Samurai supplement their swordsmanship with jujutsu to subdue foes without killing. At high levels, you can break an opponent’s arm with a flick of the wrist, or throw a heavily armoured man to the ground.

The use of the tantō (short dagger) in close combat. Includes stabbing, slashing, and defensive disarms. A secondary weapon for samurai, and the main blade of commoners allowed to carry only a small knife. In the dark corridors of a castle or the chaos of a battlefield, a quick dagger can be more lethal than a long sword.

Riding and caring for horses. Mounted samurai dominate the battlefield; this skill affects charge power, mounted archery accuracy, and the bond with your steed. A skilled rider can calm a panicked horse, jump obstacles, and fight effectively from the saddle for hours without tiring.

Swimming while wearing armour. Enables silent river crossings, escape from sinking ships, and surprising enemies from the water. A rare but life‑saving skill. At high ranks, you can hold your breath for several minutes and move silently through reeds and currents.

Operation and maintenance of the teppō (matchlock arquebus). Cleaning, loading, aiming, and firing. A new and terrifying art that can turn a peasant into a lethal soldier. High skill allows rapid reloading and accurate shooting even in wet weather when the matchcord is damp.

Directing troops using war‑fans (gunbai) and signal flags. High skill lets you issue complex formations and rally broken soldiers. The true art of the daimyō; without it, even the bravest samurai are just a disorganised mob. At master level, your presence alone can turn a rout into a victory.

Knowledge of fortification: building stone walls, moats, and keeps. Also covers siege engines, mining under walls, and defending against assaults. A master of castle design can make a small hilltop fortress impregnable, while understanding of siegecraft can starve out the strongest garrison.

Using natural features—mountains, rivers, fog—to gain an advantage in battle. Ambushes, chokepoints, and hidden paths are all revealed by this skill. A general who knows the land can defeat an army ten times his size by choosing the right ground.

Reading tracks, estimating enemy numbers, and moving unseen. Essential for gathering intelligence before a battle. High skill allows you to infiltrate enemy camps, count their banners, and return without a trace. Many battles are won before the first arrow because of a skilled scout.

Encrypting messages, using smoke signals, and understanding enemy codes. High rank lets you intercept and decipher the plans of rival clans. In an age of spies, a coded message can be worth more than a thousand soldiers.

Management of supplies: food, weapons, arrows, and horses. An army marches on its stomach, and a skilled logistician ensures it never starves. You can plan supply routes, forage efficiently, and keep morale high by preventing hunger and equipment shortages.

The shadow arts of the shinobi (ninja): stealth, disguise, and silent entry. Useful for espionage, sabotage, and escaping tight situations. High skill lets you scale walls, pick locks, and vanish into the darkness before anyone notices you were there.

Coastal raiding, ship‑to‑ship combat, and controlling the Inland Sea. Skill with boats and boarding actions. A master of naval warfare can blockade ports, capture enemy vessels, and launch surprise attacks from the sea.

Mustering armies, paying soldiers, and maintaining discipline. Essential for keeping a large force loyal and effective. High skill allows you to manage the payroll, resolve disputes among officers, and enforce laws without causing rebellion.

Understanding fear, courage, and loyalty. Inspiring your men and breaking the enemy's will without a single arrow. A leader with high seishin can turn frightened peasants into fanatical warriors, or make a proud samurai doubt his very purpose.

The backbone of Japan's economy. Knowledge of flooding paddies, planting seedlings, and managing irrigation. High yields mean wealth and influence. A master farmer can coax a bountiful harvest from the smallest plot of land, ensuring his village never goes hungry.

Growing millet, barley, soybeans, and vegetables on unflooded fields. Provides variety in diet and trade goods. Skilled dry‑field farmers know how to rotate crops, enrich soil without flooding, and produce reliable harvests even in poor weather.

Cultivating persimmons, plums, chestnuts, and citrus trees. Fruit is a luxury, and orchards can become valuable assets. High skill allows grafting, pest control, and preserving fruit for winter markets.

Harvesting timber sustainably, making charcoal, and foraging for mushrooms and wild herbs. The forest is a provider. A skilled forester knows which trees to cut and which to leave, ensuring a constant supply of wood for fuel and building.

Raising oxen, horses, chickens, and sometimes goats. Draft animals are vital for ploughing and transport. High skill leads to healthier, stronger animals that fetch higher prices and work longer hours.
Rearing silkworms on mulberry groves. Producing raw silk is a delicate and profitable art. Master sericulturists can produce the finest thread, which can be woven into garments worthy of a daimyō.

River traps, net fishing, and coastal netting. The sea and rivers provide food when the fields fail. A skilled fisherman knows the spawning seasons, the best bait, and how to read the water to find the richest catches.

Designing channels, dikes, and water‑lifting devices. Proper irrigation can turn barren land into fertile paddies. High skill allows you to move water over hills and store it for dry seasons, making your village resistant to drought.

Understanding fertilisation, crop rotation, and the health of the earth. Healthy soil means healthy crops and fewer famines. A master of dochi can rejuvenate exhausted fields and identify the best crops for each type of soil.

Selling your yield at market. Reading prices, knowing when to hold or sell, and dealing with merchants to get the best return for your labour. High skill allows you to anticipate price changes and strike bargains that enrich your household.

Forging blades, tools, nails, and plowshares. The heart of a village; a skilled blacksmith can turn raw iron into a masterpiece sword. High rank unlocks the secrets of folding steel, creating weapons that are both razor‑sharp and incredibly resilient.

Building temples, houses, bridges, and the intricate joinery that requires no nails. A master carpenter is never out of work. High skill allows you to construct structures that withstand earthquakes and typhoons, and to carve beauty into every beam.

Creating Bizen, Shigaraki, and Seto wares. From humble rice bowls to exquisite tea ceremony vessels. A master potter can shape clay into art that lasts for centuries, and his glazes are sought after by collectors across the land.

Working with hemp, silk, and indigo to create cloth of all kinds. High skill enables complex patterns and vibrant colours that do not fade. A skilled weaver can produce garments for both peasants and princes, and his workshop is always busy.
Crafting armour components, saddles, pouches, and drums from tanned hides. Leather is durable and flexible; a master leatherworker can make a suit of armour that is both light and protective.

Making washi paper for screens, scrolls, lanterns, and letters. Paper is precious in this era; a skilled papermaker can produce sheets so fine they are sought by calligraphers and government officials alike.
Cutting and fitting stone for castle walls, grave markers, and grinding stones. A master stonemason can shape a boulder into a perfect block, and his work will stand for generations.
Applying lacquer sap to wood and other surfaces to create lustrous, durable vessels and armour. High skill yields pieces with intricate gold‑leaf decoration that are treasured by the nobility.

Fermenting rice into sake, the nectar of gods and merchants. A master brewer knows the precise timing and temperature for each stage of brewing, producing a drink that warms the spirit and loosens tongues.

Crafting tsuba (sword guards), kozuka (small knives), and intricate fittings. This is the art of miniature beauty; high skill lets you carve delicate patterns that are as functional as they are beautiful.

Using the abacus and keeping ledgers. A merchant who cannot count is a merchant who will soon be poor. High skill allows you to spot errors, calculate interest, and manage the finances of an entire trading house.
Haggling in the market, striking long‑term contracts, and persuading stubborn partners. A master negotiator can turn a loss into a profit with just a few words.
Assessing the true worth of goods and spotting counterfeits. A skilled appraiser knows the difference between a genuine Ming vase and a clever forgery, and can smell out watered‑down sake.

Finding rare goods and building supplier networks. High skill allows you to source the finest silk from distant provinces, or the purest iron for a master blacksmith, at prices that still leave a profit.
Navigating highways, avoiding bandits, and paying the right tolls. A master route planner can move a caravan across the eight provinces safely and quickly, ensuring goods arrive fresh.
Understanding the confusing coinage of rival clans and exchanging copper, silver, and gold without losing value. High skill protects you from debased currency and unscrupulous moneylenders.
Storing goods safely, managing inventory, and preventing rot. A well‑run warehouse is the backbone of a trading empire; a master warehouseman can keep rice fresh for years.
Betting on harvest futures, war shortages, and seasonal shifts. High risk, high reward. A successful speculator can amass wealth overnight, but a wrong bet can lead to ruin.
Managing porters, packhorses, and river boats. Efficient transport is the lifeblood of trade. High skill reduces losses and delays, keeping your goods moving and your customers happy.
Building a reputation of reliability. Loans and favours are only extended to those who repay. High skill allows you to borrow large sums at low interest and call in debts without making enemies.
The art of persuasive speech, both formal and intimate. A silver tongue can open doors that swords cannot. High skill allows you to sway a magistrate, calm a mob, or win the heart of a noble.

Knowing the correct bows, seating arrangements, and gift‑giving rituals. A breach of etiquette can be a mortal insult. A master of gishiki moves through the highest circles with grace, never giving offence.
Reading the mood of a room and sensing others' feelings. High skill tells you when to speak, when to be silent, and when to leave altogether.
The comic arts — lightening moods, defusing wrath, and making allies through laughter. A well‑timed joke can break a tense negotiation or turn an enemy into a friend.
Remaining calm under insult, threat, or unbearable grief. A composed person betrays no weakness. High skill makes you appear unshakable, even when your heart is pounding.
Forming genuine, unbreakable relationships. Beyond casual friendship, this skill forges loyalty that lasts a lifetime. Your closest companions will follow you into death.
Spinning tales by the hearth, making memories into legends. A good storyteller is welcome everywhere, and his stories can inspire, warn, or simply entertain.
Providing food, drink, and comfort that guests never forget. A great host can win allies at the dinner table better than a general on the battlefield.
Resolving disputes between neighbours, vassals, or lords. A skilled mediator finds compromises that leave both sides satisfied, preventing feuds that could last generations.
Passing skills to others with patience and clarity. A great teacher transforms novices into masters, and his students become his legacy.
Understanding the language of law, history, and high culture. All official documents are written in Chinese characters; this skill grants access to knowledge that commoners cannot fathom.
Crafting 31‑syllable poems that move hearts and win patrons. A single elegant poem can earn a lifetime of favour from a cultured lord.
Knowing the rises and falls of clans, the precedents, and the warnings of the past. Those who forget history are doomed to repeat it.
Knowledge of the Jōei Shikimoku, local ordinances, and the rights of different classes. A skilled lawyer can argue a case before a magistrate and protect the innocent (or the guilty).
Reading the stars for navigation, planting calendars, and omens. Eclipses and comets are messages from heaven; understanding them can avert disaster.
Herbal remedies, acupuncture, and the humors of the body. A skilled physician can heal the sick, set broken bones, and recognise the early signs of plague.
The brush as the soul. Beautiful characters speak of refinement, and a well‑written letter can be as powerful as a spoken word.
Knowledge of the sixty‑six provinces, their roads, their products, and their dangers. A geographer knows the shortest path and the safest pass.
Sutras, meditation, and the doctrines of Zen and Pure Land sects. Deep knowledge brings inner peace and respect from the religious community.
Almanacs, lucky directions, and protection from malign spirits. An onmyōji can determine the best day for a wedding, or the worst place to build a house.
Knowing which plants are edible, medicinal, or poisonous. This skill can keep you alive in the wilderness when food runs out, and help you find natural cures.
Trapping, tracking, and killing game from hares to wild boar. A skilled hunter never goes hungry, and his furs can be traded for other necessities.
Moving safely in darkness, reading the moon and shadows. High skill lets you navigate by the stars and avoid the things that prowl after sunset.
Crossing passes, finding shelter, and avoiding wolves and bandits in high country. The mountains are unforgiving; this skill teaches you how to read weather signs and find safe paths.
Starting a fire in wind, rain, or snow. Fire is warmth, light, and protection; a master fire‑maker can spark a blaze with nothing but sticks and moss.
Waking at the slightest threat. Even in deep slumber, a part of your mind listens. High skill ensures you are never caught off guard by an ambush.
Pushing through hunger, exhaustion, and pain. The will to keep moving when others collapse. A master of endurance can march for days without rest.
Hiding yourself, your camp, and your tracks. In hostile territory, being invisible is more important than being strong.
Stanching wounds, setting bones, and treating fever with whatever nature provides. This skill can save a life when no physician is near.
Recognising, preparing, and countering toxins. From snake venom to hemlock, a master of poisons can kill silently or save a victim with the right antidote.
Clearing the mind, seeking truth, and resisting fear and illusion. Regular meditation can calm the spirit and grant insights that logic cannot reach.
The Pure Land chant, offering comfort to the dying and hope to the humble. Repetition of the nembutsu can bring peace in the face of suffering.
The way of the yamabushi, seeking power through ordeal. Fasting, standing under waterfalls, and enduring the cold forge the spirit into something unbreakable.
Maintaining a kami's dwelling, performing purification rites, and assisting worshippers. A dedicated shrine servant earns the respect of the community and the favour of the spirits.
Calling blessings on crops, births, battles, and journeys. The right prayer at the right time can bring good fortune or ward off disaster.
Appeasing the restless dead and understanding the near world. A person skilled in this art can exorcise a haunting or deliver a final message to a departed soul.
Knowledge of oni, tengu, kappa, and things that stalk the night. Knowing a creature's weakness can mean the difference between survival and a gruesome death.
Siting buildings, graves, and camps in harmony with the dragon lines of the earth. Proper feng shui brings prosperity; ignoring it invites disaster.
Receiving the Buddhist precepts as a layperson. This formal commitment brings moral authority and the support of the temple.
The sheer force of spirit that bends fate, resists curses, and inspires armies. A strong will can overcome physical weakness and achieve the impossible.
Preparing matcha with grace, the hut, the garden, the moment. The tea ceremony is an act of mindfulness and beauty; masters of sado are sought after by daimyō as cultural advisors.
Bringing the mountain and season into a single alcove. A master of ikebana can evoke an entire landscape with three blossoms and a twisted branch.
The masked drama of ghosts, gods, and the sorrow of warriors. Performing Noh requires years of training; a skilled actor can move an audience to tears without a single word.
The narrative ballads of the Heike, strummed and sung. The biwa is the voice of history; its music recounts the rise and fall of the great clans.
The sound of Zen, of wind through pines, of solitude. The shakuhachi is used by wandering monks as a form of meditation; its haunting notes can calm a troubled mind.
Landscapes, birds, the fleeting beauty of the world in sumi‑e. A master painter captures the essence of a subject with a few brushstrokes.
The discerning nose, the game of fragrant woods. Incense ceremonies are a mark of high culture; a master of kodo can identify dozens of scents blindfolded.
Composing poetry with companions, each verse building on the last. Renga is a social art that requires wit, harmony, and deep cultural knowledge.
The art of karesansui, the dry landscape. Raking gravel into ripples, placing stones to suggest mountains and islands. A master garden can be meditated upon for hours.
Weaving multiple arts into a single, elegant life. A true polymath who excels in several disciplines is the ideal of the cultured warrior — the bunbu ryōdō (pen and sword in accord).
